issues.title: Turning off DTLS replay protection is typically a major security problem
issues.author: emanjon
issues.body:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/tls/f990CfKIRDQKTa4tAkIrgHcKqWc/

---------------------------

Hi,

Reading RFC 9147 (DTLS 1.3) I cannot find any other interpretation than that replay protection may be disabled for all records. This is not a problem for the initial lock-step handshake, alerts, KeyUpdate, and ACKs. It seems to be a major problem for NewSessionTicket, NewConnectionId, RequestConnectionId, and Post-handshake client authentication as the lack of replay protection might significantly affect availability. It seems to me that DTLS 1.3 forgot to update replay protection based on the new post-handshake messages. Let me know if I miss something.

It is a bit surprising that DTLS 1.3 published in 2022 allows the application to turn off replay protection at all. This very far from current best practice for security protocols. There are very good reasons why Datagram QUIC mandates replay protection and why TLS 1.3 has several pages discussing security considerations for 0-RTT data, which lacks replay protection. In general, turning off replay protection (even just for application data) might lead to loss of confidentiality, integrity, and availability, i.e., the whole CIA triad.

Applications cannot be expected to understand the severe consequences of not having replay protection or understand how to fix it on the application layer. I also don't see any need for turning off replay protection except RFC 6083 which is a bit of a special case, and which turned out to have replay issues.
https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/115/materials/slides-115-tsvwg-sctp-auth-security-issues-00

I would strongly recommend all DTLS 1.3 libraries to completely remove the option to disable replay protection.

An easy fix for the post-handshake messages is to "clarify" that replay protection must not be turned off for anything else than application data. I you agree I can submit an “erratum” for RFC 9147. But this does not solve the general issue that turning off replay protection would be a major security problem in almost all applications.

Cheers,
John Preuß Mattsson

comments.author: emanjon
comments.body: I am very sceptical to the option to turn of the DTLS replay protection. Is this needed for anything? I would recommend removing it. Otherwise a lot of analysis and considerations are likely needed.

issues.title: Are there benefits to encrypt PPID 4242 when possible
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
As currently defined the DTLS Keymanagement messages in Data chunk with PPID=4242 will always be sent unencrypted. This is simple and avoids error cases if keys expires or are removed for the DTLS Chunk. However, are there any security benefits to encrypting them when one can? It will make targeted attacks against the key-managment harder for established associations, the the actual message itself is protected, but identifiable. However, one can never prevent denial in general through packet dropping. Also it makes the key-management sensitive to synchronization issues in case of failures or key-limit expiration requiring fallback behaviors. 

So the main question is if extra complexities to have different choices and fallback handling is worth the potential limited benefit of encrypting.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Allowing unprotected packets with DATA chunks actually create one significant weakness. It allows attackers capable of injecting packets to attempt to impact the DATA chunk tracking process, inject overlapping DATA chunks with protected and impact the content of the SACK block. Thus, it is likely simplest to always require using protected packets. If there would be a serious issue with keys, one can perform a SCTP association restart using the restart DTLS session context. This is disruptive, but the failure case to not have a valid key are also fairly major and should not be happening in associations.

issues.title: Clarify the case when Endpoints allow reverting to rfc6083
issues.author: teiclap
issues.body:
It is possible that the Endpoint wants to advertise the peer that it can handle rfc6083 in INIT by using the set of INIT parameters specified in rfc6083, together with the DTLS-CHUNK.
In case the peer cannot handle DTLS-CHUNK, the Association would be set using DTLS/SCTP according to rfc6083.
Since rfc6083 has known packet size limitations and security weaknesses, we need to explain that the combination of rfc6083 as revert solution for DTLS-CHUNK is not viable and that such combination should no be accepted in any case.

In addition I think the handling of other solutions needs to be written into a procedure or new section, rather than just the security considerations on downgrade.

issues.title: Avoiding deadlock in Initial key-establishment
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
During implementation we realized we have a potential for a deadlock in the key establishment process. This would occur if the DTLS server configures and starts using the first DCI as soon as possible. With Diffie-Hellman key-exchange the client can first generate the full set of keying material when it has received the servers ServerHello message. This being delivered using unprotected DATA chunks using PPID=4242 requires that the server are certain that this has been delivered to the client before the server starts transmitting and requiring any incoming packets to be protected. 

To simplify for the server's state handling an initial proposal to deal with this is the below. So when the server has gotten far along the process to have keys, it will install the created DCI. However, it will not use it for outgoing nor require it to be used, i.e. still accept unprotected traffic. It falls on the client to not enter the Validation state and start that process until it is certain that it self and the server have the DCI in place. The server will when receiving the protected PVALID chunk move to Protected and at that stage use protected traffic going forward. 

```
     +---------------+
     |  ESTABLISHED  |
     +-------+-------+
             |
             | If INIT/INIT-ACK has DTLS 1.3 Chunk
             | Protected Association Parameter
             v
+--------------------------+
| PROTECTION INITILIZATION |
+------------+-------------+
             |
             | start T-valid timer.
             |
             | [DTLS SETUP]
             |-----------------
             | send and receive
             | DTLS handshake
             v
 +----------------------+
 |  VALIDATION INIT     |
 +-----------+----------+
             | Client and Server: Init DCI, Don't use it for transmission
	     | 
	     | Client: Go to next state
	     | Server: Wait for Protected PVALID from Client
             V
 +----------------------+
 |      VALIDATION      |
 +-----------+----------+
             | 
             | [ENDPOINT VALIDATION]
             | Require use of DTLS Chunk    
             |------------------------
             | send and receive
             | PVALID by means of
             | DTLS chunk.
             v
     +---------------+
     |   PROTECTED   | 
     +---------------+
 ```

issues.title: PVALID retransmission optimisation
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body: So when the client role sends the PVALID it will retransmit after one RTO. However, if the clients PVALID reached the server and it was the response PVALID that was lost, the client may receive additional SCTP packets with DTLS protected chunks. This indicate to the client that a likely loss of the server-> client PVALID chunk. The caveat is network reordering, thus a short delay could prevent unnecessary retransmissions. However, I don't see an issue with triggering one retransmission immediately, and restart the RTO timer. The whole goal of this is speeding up the time until the association on both sides reaches PROTECTED state.

issues.title: DT : Replace PVALID with a Data Chunk and a new PPID
issues.author: teiclap
issues.body: From the Design Team : the PVALID chunk is not a preferred solution, it shall be replaced by using a legacy DATA Chunk having a new PPID.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
@teiclap and I tried to implement the design team direction of attempting to move the downgrade attack prevention that PVALID Chunk was supporting to the security function running in user space above an SCTP stack. We have run into issues which we think are the result of not fully thinking through this direction and would like to discuss it further.
 
The main goal of the PVALID mechanism is ensure that all that was negotiated in clear text was all that the sender of the respective side actually offered so no downgrades occurred at this step INIT/INIT-ACK. To be able to do that both sides needs to accurately represent all that was offered when it comes to security parameters and compare that to all that was received on the peer side. The PVALID mechanism need to carry the relevant information of both what was offered and what was received. I think it is here that we didn’t think this through properly what the implications of this is when trying to do this from the user land level. 
 
The user land process initiating an SCTP association needs to configure the SCTP stack with which security related options it wants to offer. This will result in one or more security related parameter will be included. So here I would note that we existing parameters are DTLS Chunk (with key-management indicators) and possibly also SCTP-AUTH with the chunks/all chunks, random, HMAC Algo. These offered parameters needs to be either parameterized and included in the cookie, or simply the whole set of relevant parameters included in cookie and then encrypted. 
 
The responder sends back the INIT-ACK with parameter response for what it accepts to use and relevant parameter values. The Init-ACK also contains the state cookie containing the offered info as well as it’s own response of information. As the responder want to remain stateless for this first Init/Init-ACK procedure nothing is stored locally or given to the upper layer. 
 
The Initiator receives the INIT-ACK and verifies that the SCTP Association and configured security parameters is viable and stores either the parameters or a representation of what it received. Then it sends the Cookie ECHO back to the responder side. Here the information from the  INIT/INIT-ACK could be provided to key-management but it likely makes sense to wait until 5 when the SCTP association enters established.
 
The responder side receives the Cookie-ECHO and unpacks the cookie and creates the SCTP Association state and stores the security parameters. On the responder side the SCTP association is now in established state. However, the key-management step is so far not initiated by the responder but by the initiator. The responder sends the Cookie-ACK. Here is the earlies the responder side could provide the relevant information from the INIT and INIT-ACK to the key management.
 
The initiator is receiving the Cookie-ACK and goes into established state. As the SCTP Association establishment succeeded the key-management mechanism can be triggered. It will send its DTLS Client Hello. 
 
The responder receives the DTLS client hell and gives it to the key-management function. Generates the DTLS Server hello and sends that back. At this point it can install DTLS Chunk receive keys, and send keys, but can’t activate them until it has confirmed that the Server Hello has reached the DTLS Client. So technically this could be based on SCTP SACKs, but I think that is complex when it comes to the API function. Thus, one could initiate PVALID here and send an encrypted DTLS Chunk with a PVALID message, thus potentially mixing encrypted and unencrypted SCTP packets, however any retransmission of the Server Hello must be sent unencrypted.
 
The initiator receives the Server Hello and can now install both receiver keys and send keys. It now sends the DTLS Client ACK to conclude the DTLS handshake. This can be sent encrypted to my understanding. The initiator side can after having sent this initiate PVALID mechanism. 
 
The responder receives the DTLS Client ACK (encrypted) and can also verify conclusion of DTLS handshake and also move to send only encrypted at this point. At this stage it can definitely run PVALID process. 
 
Lets, now look at what information is needed to perform the PVALID mechanism. For the initiator to generate a PVALID message it needs to have the actual parameter set or a representation of that stored in the key-management user land protocol. Which means that it either can determine what would be sent based on the configuration, or need have an API function to query the SCTP stack to get what was included in the INIT. Then it could send its PVALID message with this information to the responder.
 
The responder when receiving the PVALID message needs to compare the included information with was actually received in the INIT message. However, due to the statelessness, it will get this from the Cookie-Echo (4) and thus needs an API function here to extract this information. I will also note that we need to handle the situation that the responder might not know all the parameters that was offered. Thus, it lacks implementation to know what some of these parameters are. Claudio and I concluded that this comparison can be done in two ways. The PVALID Chunk requires the SCTP stack to determine all options it received in the INIT based on its implementation capabilities and mark those down. Then when it receives the PVALID chunk it compared all it could understand, with all that is offered. If anything was offered that the responder implements but didn’t mark as parsed from the INIT we have a potential modification and should abort. Doing this from the user land level generates certain API requirements. It basically means that in a step after (4) the responder key-management side should read up the SCTP stacks interpretation of what was offered in the INIT. It also needs and API function that gives the corresponding list of all that is implemented in this stack. We see moving this information across the SCTP stack boundary up to the key-management as non-trivial and requires generating an information structure that represents all relevant security parameter combinations. 

Claudio have an alternative idea that appears to both of us to result in simpler implementation assuming that PVALID remains a chunk. The core of this idea is to have the implementations perform only a byte comparison between what it received in the INIT/INIT-ACK and what is contained in the PVALID Chunk. We think only the Parameters needs to be compared. So the PVALID chunk will contain a full binary representation of the INIT or INIT-ACK parameters depending on direction. That way both sides simply save what it sent, and later sends it in the PVALID Chunk. As this binary byte object is not interpreted, only stored as part of the SCTP association state doing the validation within the SCTP level is simple and straight forward and avoids the need for additional API calls. 
 
The only potential downside with this is that this will grow the handshake messages, especially the INIT-ACK as that message needs to contain both the parameter response in clear text, as well as both the received parameters from the INIT as well as its response but protected inside the encrypted Cookie. Thus, the INIT-ACK will contain three sets of parameters.
 
The design team will discuss this further.

We plan to send the PVALID messages over seperate PPID.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
I think I have an idea how to simplify this:
* The client sends one or more protection solution Ids in the protection solutions parameter.
* The server sends exactly one  protection solution Id in the protection solutions parameter. This is one of the received ones.
* Provide an API to let the application know which protection solutions parameter was sent and received.
* After the association is established the communication is unprotected. This allows the DTLS handshake to happen.
* When deriving the send/recv keys, make them include the protection solutions parameters during key derivation.
* This way the protected packets are also protected against downgrade attacks and we don't need any additional message exchanges. 
* Once you provided a receive key, no unprotected packets will be processed anymore. 
* Or am I missing something, @gloinul @hannestschofenig

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
I think including the protection solution as context value in the TLS key exporter is potentially a good idea if one was expecting to use the same labels for DTLS based key-exchanges. However, I think per key-managment solution unique labels would have the same properties. I think the important here is that two different key-managmenet specs (and thus indicated protection solutions) would not result in the same actual Traffic keys. 

So, I am a bit hesitant against having no limitation on unprotected traffic prior to key-installment if I understand the implications of bullet four. So it would be volunteer to use PPID=4242 for this. Do you expect to integrate the key-management into ones ULP also with this proposal? 

Also having worked on Ericsson's handshake draft I am more and more desiring to go back to the PVALID Chunk for validation. This as the timing of the exchange and how to handle that requires further interaction and puts the requirements on the key-management. I rather do it in on place, i.e. as part of chunk.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
I am considering the man-in-the-middle case where the downgrade happens by modification of INIT chunk so that the server will never be aware of the whole set of the protection solutions.
The client's API will never know why the server has chosen a certain solution unless a dedicated, protected handshake exists.
I may have missed some parts of the reasoning, but I am still convinced that an handshake for validation is needed.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
> I am considering the man-in-the-middle case where the downgrade happens by modification of INIT chunk so that the server will never be aware of the whole set of the protection solutions. The client's API will never know why the server has chosen a certain solution unless a dedicated, protected handshake exists. I may have missed some parts of the reasoning, but I am still convinced that an handshake for validation is needed.

Assume the client puts PSID1 and PSID2 in the protection solution parameter, the attacker remove PSID1 and therefore the server only sees PSID2. Then the server responds with PSID2.
Now
* the client would use PSID1 and PSID2 for the client information and PSID2 for the server one in the key derivation
* the server would use PSID2 for the client information and PSID2 for the server on.
Therefore the keys are different and no validated information would be transferred.

comments.body:
> I think including the protection solution as context value in the TLS key exporter is potentially a good idea if one was expecting to use the same labels for DTLS based key-exchanges. The PPID should not be looked or modified by the SCTP stack. At least not in the base stack.
Since we want to be generic on the key management, I thought the SCTP stack will provide the information with each user message if the user message was received in a protected way or not.
This way the upper layer can check that its constraints are fulfilled.

> Do you expect to integrate the key-management into ones ULP also with this proposal?

I am not sure what this sentence means.
We need to wait with the downgrade protection until we can communicate in a protected way. Then we could define a simple protocol to ensure there was no downgrade and ensure that we know this before processing protected user messages. But building this step in the key derivation, which we wanted to include in the key management document anyway, sees a very clear and simple solution.

It enforces that no protected user data is delivered in case of a downgrade attack.
> 
> Also having worked on Ericsson's handshake draft I am more and more desiring to go back to the PVALID Chunk for validation. This as the timing of the exchange and how to handle that requires further interaction and puts the requirements on the key-management. I rather do it in on place, i.e. as part of chunk.

I don't follow here. You need to derive the transmission keys for the DTLD chunk. Just incorporate the information from the SCTP handshake.

However, this makes the implementation much more complex. You need another chunk with retransmission stuff, change the state engine, ...

Putting this into the key derivation, we would need to implement
* a socket option which enforces the drop of unprotected packets
* the sending and receiving of the protection solution parameter
* the corresponding socket option for handling them
* the socket options to provide the traffic and restart keys
* the sending and receiving of the DTLS chunk
* the exposure of user messages have been received in a protected way or not

Add of these seems to be limited functional changes that can be reviewed and tested even with pre-shared keys.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
Ok, your clarification around wanting to include the whole set of offered solutions into context of the key derivation makes a difference. It still results in the chunk putting recommendation to the key-management. 

I think what is needed in regards to these proposal is what impact it has to state machine and abstract API functions. @tuexen can you please put a bit more details into your proposal so that we really can see what it entails and what then ends up being expected to be done by the key-management. 

I think there is an issue with one line of your strawman:

- Once you provided a receive key, no unprotected packets will be processed anymore.

I think this can result in the deadlock situations unless there is a separate call for moving to require all traffic to be encrypted.

The failure case which I don't see working is this:
1: A->Z: DTLS CH
2: Z->A: DTLS SH
3: Z: Can derive both keys, only sets READ as 2 needs to be delivered as plain text. If packet loss of part of (2), SACK will be sent as plain text A->Z. 
4: A: Derives Read and Write, sets both (
5: A->Z: P(DTLS ACK)
6: Z: Sets Write key and locks down receiving unencrypted.
7: Z->A: Send P(PVALID message)
8: A: Locks down to only receive protected.
9: A->Z: Send P(PVALID message)

There are alternatives of this, but there appear to be cases where at least one side needs to be able to receive protected, but can't guarantee that all will be protected as there might be unprotected SACK chunks requesting restransmission. And until the next message has been received one can't commit to writing secure as it would cause deadlock.

issues.title: When performing SCTP Association restart do one reset epoch?
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
Do one reset the DTLS epoch values after an SCTP assocation restart protected by the DTLS chunk?

As the DTLS 1.3 epoch counter is 64-bit I don't think there are a real issue of running out of space and do not need to be reset for that reason. If one reset the counter, the node not restarting could potentially be forced to overwrite the current epoch. However, the SCTP association restart basically result in a new DTLS key context store, which will initially only contain the restart DTLS context ID. There should be no traffic DTLS key context, these should only be generated after a new DTLS key management invocation. 

If one do not reset the counter then there will be a need to for the key-management on the restarting side to understand that the next epoch value to use is the one used by the restart +1.

Do anyone see issues with reseting epoch on restart?

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: As both side knows the restart key used for the restart using restart +1 is actually fine, and might have less overlap issues compared to being on the first epoch and resetting it back and then having to overwrite the restart key used, after having installed the traffic key. In some particular situation we might end up overwriting an existing key in cases if the restart happens in the exact moment the negotiation when the non restarting have managed to install the next key, but not the crashing side.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Design Team discussion: Assumption will reset it to value=3 after restart. The API needs to take into account that full epoch resets from higher value to a lower.

comments.body: Discussion: ACONF from new address when encapsulated will not work. Using it encapsulated when just signalling a new address over an existing one will work. Explain this limitation.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
ASCONF from new address when encapsulated brings the Verification Tag, this can be used for properly addressing the right Association. There's a chance that VTag is not unique, this can be solved either by sending the packet to one of the matching Associations in a sequential mode when the ASCONF for that VTag is received next, or to broadcast the message.
The lower part of SCTP doesn't know that the message is ASCONF until it's successfully decrypted and on the other hand broadcasting the message to all the matching Association my open a little chance for Denial Of Service.
As a packet not being decrypted will not generate any side effect, I would make as follows:
1. A packet that has a known VTag but unknown Source Address will be delivered to one of the Association with matching VTag and a sequence will be kept by SCTP to send the retransmission to the next and so on.
2. If decrypting of the packet resuts in ASCONF, the rfc5061 is followed.
3. We write in this draft that SCTP-AUTH is not followed.

issues.title: Motivation of SCTP restart
issues.author: tuexen
issues.body:
- Motivation unclear
- How can the restart key be stored in a secure way
- Mandatory to implement
- What happens if not implemented

This is to be discussed at the next VC.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
I assign this to myself and will work on clarify the motivation.

We should have an indication whether we support restart or not, so that no attempts for restart are tried if not supported and a new Association is eventually created. Since restart is an important Use Case in 3GPP, we would RECOMMEND it to be implemented.

On storing the Key Materials in a secure way, this is up to the implementation. It may be stored by the application, by a secure storage service or in any other way.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
### Motivation for SCTP Restart

In a hierarchical network, where Central Nodes are connected to access nodes via SCTP Association.
Users are tied to access network nodes.
When an access node realizes that the core network node it depends, is not accessible, it must shutdown as it's out of control.
Central Nodes are quick at restarting, normally faster than the SCTP supervision time at the access nodes.
The SCTP Restart feature is used by Core Network nodes to recover the control on the access nodes so that the Users won't experience the network outage. 
A network outage due to restart of a core network node can potentially impact a very large number of Users.

issues.title: Padding of SCTP Packets
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
As both SCTP and DTLS can add padding to achieve a certain packet size I think further text and clarification of this should be added. The following text was present in draft-westerlund-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-handshake but it makes more sense to add it to Chunks instead. Which leads to an additional question if there should be an API function to instruct the DTLS Chunk to always produce a multiple of the set size large DTLS Chunks. 
```

Both SCTP and DTLS contains mechanisms to padd SCTP payloads, and DTLS
records respectively. If padding of SCTP packets are desired to hide
actual message sizes it RECOMMEDED to use the SCTP Padding Chunck
{{RFC4820}} to generate a consistent SCTP payload size. Support of
this chunk is only required on the sender side, any SCTP receiver will
safely ignore the PAD Chunk. However, if the PAD chunk is not
supported DTLS padding MAY be used.

It needs to be noted that independent if SCTP padding or DTLS padding
is used the padding is not taken into account by the SCTP congestion
control. Extensive use of padding has potential for worsen congestion
situations as the SCTP association will consume more bandwidth than
its derived share by the congestion control.

The use of SCTP PAD chunk is recommened as it at least can enable
future extension or SCTP implementation that account also for the
padding. Use of DTLS padding hides this packet expansion from SCTP.
```

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
Okay, I think you are right in that something have to be done here. As we do have a mismatch due to this keying. 

So Section 7.3 of RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3) is defining how one take the traffic secret to derive write_key and write_iv per direction.

> [[sender](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#ref-sender)]_write_key = HKDF-Expand-Label(Secret, "key", "", key_length)
>   [[sender](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#ref-sender)]_write_iv  = HKDF-Expand-Label(Secret, "iv", "", iv_length)
>
>   [sender] denotes the sending side.  The value of Secret for each
>   record type is shown in the table below.
>
>       +-------------------+---------------------------------------+
>       | Record Type       | Secret                                |
>       +-------------------+---------------------------------------+
>       | 0-RTT Application | client_early_traffic_secret           |
>       |                   |                                       |
>       | Handshake         | [[sender](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#ref-sender)]_handshake_traffic_secret     |
>       |                   |                                       |
>       | Application Data  | [[sender](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446.html#ref-sender)]_application_traffic_secret_N |
>       +-------------------+---------------------------------------+

Thus to get the client_sn_key one need to traffic secret for the client. 

I think this results in that we either need to expand the API as you say to include the client_sn_key and server_sn_key, alternatively we could redefine the API to take a traffic secret, and define that the SCTP chunk implementation under the API will perform the derivations for the write_key, the write_iv (per 7.3 of TLS 1.3) and the sn_key per 4.2.3 (DTLS 1.3).

issues.title: get Q (Protection Invocations) for incoming packets
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body: The current API defines get Q which counts the number of invocations of the cipher suit for a given epoch being sent by the endpoint. So the question, are there a point of counting the same value for incoming? This value will clearly just be an estimate of the senders side, as it will never be larger, but likely lower due to packet loss (assuming replay protection).

comments.author: emanjon
comments.body:
Yes, I think it would make sense to have APIs to retrieve:

1. the number of AEAD encryption invocations
2. the number of AEAD decryption invocations
3. the number of failed AEAD decryption invocations

FYI, we recently looked into how RFC 8446 and OpenSSL handles rekeying:
 
"While RFC 8446 specifies that implementations SHOULD perform a key update before reaching 2^24.5 full-size records equaling 2^34.5 blocks, the key update mechanism is much weaker than the mechanism required in the SSH profile, and does not protect against key leakage. Furthermore RFC 8446, and to our understanding OpenSSL, does not mandate rekeying before reaching 2^64 records equaling up to 7^24 blocks, or theoretically even more in the case of very large TLS records [24]. In OpenSSL, compliance to any rekeying limit is left entirely to the application, which is poor security design. We recommend that the CNSA profile mandate that TLS 1.3 implementations enforce a 2^59 block limit [21–23]. A compliant implementation should be required to automatically trigger a key update or terminate the connection before the limit is reached, placing the enforcement burden on the protocol implementation, not the application developer. Frequent rekeying with ephemeral asymmetric key exchange, similar to the requirements for SSH and IPsec, and which can be achieved with resumption, should be recommended."

https://emanjon.github.io/CNSA-comments/2025%20-%20Comments%20on%20CNSA%202.0%20drafts.pdf

My understanding is that OpenSSL does not even have an "get Q" interface. The application has to count the number of bytes it inputs to TLS.

issues.title: The Key Context Information is inconsistent
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
So Section 7 says:

This API enables the cryptographical protection operations by setting client/server write keys, sequence number keys, and IVs for primary and restart DTLS contexts. The write key is used by the cipher suite for DTLS record protection ([Section 5.2](https://rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.2) of [[RFC8446](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-chunk-02.html#RFC8446)]). The initialization vector (IV) is random material used to XOR with the sequence number to create the nonce per [Section 5.3](https://rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8446#section-5.3) of [[RFC8446](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-chunk-02.html#RFC8446)]. The sequence number key is used to encrypt the sequence number ([Section 4.2.3](https://rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9147#section-4.2.3) of [[RFC9147](https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-chunk-02.html#RFC9147)]).

Section 3.1 says:

In this document we use the terms DTLS Key context for indicating a Key and IV, produced by the DTLS Key Management, and all relevant data that needs to be provided to the Chunk Protection Operator for DTLS encryption and decryption. DTLS Key context includes Keys and IV for sending and receiving, replay window, last used sequence number. Each DTLS key context is associated with a three-value tuple identifying the context, consisting of SCTP Association, the restart indicator, and the DTLS epoch.

These definitions and naming of the DTLS Key Context is not conistent as the one in 3.1 lacks the sequence number protection key. So we need to address this.

issues.title: Clarify who has the DTLS Client role when SCTP Simultanous open happens
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
When setting the DTLS Key Context it is very important that both side agrees which endpoint that has the DTLS Client Role and who has the server role so the right set of keys are installed. 

As SCTP do support simultanous open, i.e. when both sides sends INIT at the same time there need to be clarified who gets the DTLS client/server role in this case.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body: I think that the role for DTLS should follow the role SCTP gets after solving the race condition

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Can you @teiclap point to any text in an RFC that actually makes this role clear after an simultanous open. I looked quickly and didn't find anything in rfc9260 that the resulting role clear, only that this is part of the exception processing and how to resolve this into one association. We will need some text for this that point to the relevant part of the SCTP specifications on how to arrive at this role.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
Hi Magnus,
The solution uses the concept of Tie-Tags.

I’ve used AI for creating a summary.

Regards,
Claudio


RFC 9260 explicitly defines how the “simultaneous INIT” race condition is detected and resolved, and it is handled under Section 5.2.1 (“INIT Chunk Received in COOKIE‑WAIT or COOKIE‑ECHOED State”). This is the SCTP equivalent of a simultaneous open in TCP, but with SCTP‑specific resolution logic.

Below is a precise, RFC‑faithful explanation, with no extrapolation beyond the text.

________________________________
1. What is the race condition?

The race occurs when both endpoints send an INIT at roughly the same time, i.e.:

  *   A sends INIT to Z
  *   Z sends INIT to A
  *   Each endpoint receives an INIT while already trying to establish the association

RFC 9260 explicitly classifies this as case (B) in Section 5.2.

________________________________
2. Where this is defined in RFC 9260

Section 5.2.1 – “INIT Chunk Received in COOKIE‑WAIT or COOKIE‑ECHOED State (Item B)”

This section is entirely dedicated to resolving simultaneous INITs.

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
3. Detection of the race

An endpoint detects a simultaneous INIT when:

  *   It is in COOKIE‑WAIT or COOKIE‑ECHOED state
  *   It receives an INIT chunk from the peer

This combination is defined by the RFC as an initialization collision.

“This usually indicates an initialization collision, i.e., each endpoint is attempting, at about the same time, to establish an association with the other endpoint.”

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
4. Resolution rules (normative behavior)
Case A — INIT received while in COOKIE‑WAIT

Mandatory behavior:

  1.  Respond with INIT ACK
  2.  Use the same parameters as the original INIT
     *   Initiate Tag MUST NOT change
  3.  Generate a new State Cookie
  4.  Do NOT change state
  5.  Do NOT destroy the TCB
  6.  Leave T1‑init running

Key quote (paraphrased precisely):

The endpoint MUST respond with an INIT ACK chunk using the same parameters it sent in its original INIT chunk (including its Initiate Tag, unchanged).

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
Case B — INIT received while in COOKIE‑ECHOED

Same handling unless new addresses are introduced:

  *   If no new addresses → same behavior as COOKIE‑WAIT
  *   If new addresses are added → INIT is rejected

If new addresses appear:

  *   Discard INIT
  *   Send ABORT
  *   Include “Restart of an Association with New Addresses” error cause

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
5. Why this works (important insight grounded in RFC)

The race is resolved because:

  *   Both sides continue their original INIT attempt
  *   Verification Tags are preserved
  *   State Cookies carry tie‑breaking information
  *   Final resolution happens during COOKIE ECHO processing

The RFC is explicit:

“The normal procedures for handling State Cookies when a TCB exists will resolve the duplicate INIT chunks to a single association.”

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

So the collision is not resolved immediately at INIT time, but deterministically later, when cookies are validated.

________________________________
6. Tie‑Tags: the deterministic tie‑breaker

When simultaneous INITs occur, SCTP uses Tie‑Tags (Section 5.2.2 / 5.2.4):

  *   Two random 32‑bit values
  *   Stored both:
     *   In the TCB
     *   In the State Cookie
  *   Used to correlate restarted / collided associations

Tie‑Tags ensure:

  *   No duplicate associations
  *   No ambiguity after both sides exchange cookies

[rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
7. Final outcome guarantee

RFC 9260 guarantees that:

  *   ✅ Only one SCTP association survives
  *   ✅ Both endpoints converge to ESTABLISHED
  *   ✅ No resources are leaked
  *   ✅ No symmetry deadlock exists

This is stronger than TCP’s simultaneous open handling, because SCTP remains stateless until cookie validation.

________________________________
8. Minimal sequence diagram (race case)

A                                   Z

---------------------------------------

INIT (Tag_A) ----------->

                   <----------- INIT (Tag_Z)



INIT ACK (Tag_A, Cookie_A) <---------

                   ---------> INIT ACK (Tag_Z, Cookie_Z)



COOKIE ECHO (Cookie_Z) ---->

                   <---- COOKIE ECHO (Cookie_A)



COOKIE ACK <---------------

                   --------------> COOKIE ACK



Association ESTABLISHED (single)

The cookie + tie‑tag logic collapses this into one association.

________________________________
9. Key takeaway (one‑sentence answer)

RFC 9260 resolves simultaneous INIT races by replying with INIT ACK using unchanged parameters, deferring resolution to State Cookie and Tie‑Tag processing, which deterministically collapses both attempts into a single association.


About Tie-Tags


Tie‑Tags are a narrowly scoped but crucial mechanism in RFC 9260, and their purpose, meaning, and usage are defined explicitly and normatively in a few specific places. Below I’ll walk through this in a structured, RFC‑accurate way, focusing on what Tie‑Tags are, where their meaning is defined, when they are used, and how they resolve races/restarts—without adding behavior not stated in the RFC.

________________________________
1. Where Tie‑Tags are defined (normative definition)
Primary definition — Section 1.3 (Key Terms)

RFC 9260 defines Tie‑Tags explicitly as a protocol concept:

Tie‑Tags: Two 32‑bit random numbers that together make a 64‑bit nonce.
These tags are used within a State Cookie and TCB so that a newly restarting association can be linked to the original association within the endpoint that did not restart and yet not reveal the true Verification Tags of an existing association. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

Important points from the definition:

  *   Tie‑Tags are not Verification Tags
  *   They are:
     *   Random
     *   Paired (local + peer)
     *   Used only for correlation, not packet validation
  *   Their purpose is association disambiguation, not security enforcement

This is the semantic anchor for Tie‑Tags in the RFC.

________________________________
2. Where Tie‑Tags are introduced into protocol operation

Tie‑Tags are not present during a normal, collision‑free startup.

They are introduced only when an INIT is received while an association (or partial association) already exists.

Section 5.2.1 — simultaneous INIT (collision)

When an endpoint receives an INIT while in:

  *   COOKIE-WAIT, or
  *   COOKIE-ECHOED

the RFC mandates that:

For an endpoint that is in the COOKIE‑ECHOED state, it MUST populate its Tie‑Tags within both the association TCB and inside the State Cookie. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

Key implications:

  *   Tie‑Tags are generated only in collision/restart scenarios
  *   They are stored in two places:
     *   The existing TCB
     *   The newly generated State Cookie

This ensures later correlation.

________________________________
3. Where Tie‑Tags are used (actual decision logic)
Section 5.2.4 — “Handle a COOKIE ECHO Chunk When a TCB Exists”

This is the core operational use of Tie‑Tags.

When a COOKIE ECHO arrives and a TCB already exists, the endpoint:

  1.  Authenticates the cookie
  2.  Extracts Tie‑Tags from:
     *   The cookie
     *   The existing TCB
  3.  Compares:
     *   Local Tag
     *   Peer’s Tag
     *   Local‑Tie‑Tag
     *   Peer‑Tie‑Tag

The RFC then defines Table 12, which maps these comparisons to deterministic actions. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
4. What Tie‑Tags mean (their semantic role)

Tie‑Tags answer one very specific question:

Is this COOKIE ECHO logically related to my current association attempt, or is it stale / competing / from a restarted peer?

They do not:

  *   Authenticate packets
  *   Replace Verification Tags
  *   Select “winner” by numeric ordering

Instead, they:

  *   Provide continuity identity across:
     *   Simultaneous INIT collisions
     *   Association restarts
  *   Without exposing real Verification Tags

This is why the RFC explicitly says Tie‑Tags avoid revealing verification tags while still allowing linkage. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
5. How Tie‑Tags resolve the race (step‑by‑step logic)
Typical simultaneous INIT resolution

  1.  Both endpoints send INIT
  2.  Both generate INIT ACK + State Cookie
  3.  Tie‑Tags are generated and embedded
  4.  COOKIE ECHO messages cross
  5.  On receipt:
     *   Endpoint compares Tie‑Tags from cookie vs TCB
  6.  Exactly one association survives, per Table 12

RFC guarantee:

The normal procedures for handling State Cookies when a TCB exists will resolve the duplicate INIT chunks to a single association. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

Tie‑Tags are the data that makes this deterministic.

________________________________
6. Tie‑Tags vs Verification Tags (important distinction)
Aspect
Verification Tag
Tie‑Tag
Size
32‑bit
64‑bit (2×32)
Visible on wire
Yes (common header)
Only inside cookies
Purpose
Packet association validation
Association correlation
Lifetime
Entire association
Collision/restart window
Security role
Prevent blind injection
Prevent ambiguity

This distinction is explicitly implied by how the RFC scopes Tie‑Tags. [rfc9260 | Txt]<https://ericsson-my.sharepoint.com/personal/claudio_porfiri_ericsson_com/Documents/Microsoft%20Copilot%20Chat%20Files/rfc9260.txt>

________________________________
7. Why Tie‑Tags are necessary (RFC rationale, implicit but clear)

Without Tie‑Tags:

  *   Simultaneous INITs could create:
     *   Duplicate associations
     *   Verification‑tag confusion
  *   Restarted endpoints could:
     *   Be mistaken for attackers
     *   Or cause false aborts

Tie‑Tags allow SCTP to:

  *   Remain stateless until COOKIE ECHO
  *   Resolve races without global ordering
  *   Avoid TCP‑style simultaneous‑open ambiguity

________________________________
8. One‑sentence authoritative summary

In RFC 9260, Tie‑Tags are a pair of random 32‑bit values stored in both the TCB and State Cookie, defined in Section 1.3 and used in Sections 5.2.1 and 5.2.4 to deterministically correlate simultaneous INITs or restarts into a single SCTP association without exposing Verification Tags.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
The keys have to be provided in a consistent way at the API layer. I don't see it bound to client or server role. The key management methods (both the Ericsson one and the IETF one) run a DTLS connection. At that layer the client and server role is clear.
At the SCTP layer you can use client code on both sides, but not at the DTLS layer. So for the key management solutions it is clear. Therefore, I don't understand which problem needs to be solved. Could you elaborate on that?

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
The core of the problem is that the key-management then will have to replicate the tie-breaker mechanism. At least for 3GPP Xn interface we have the potential that two gNB at the same time needs to create an SCTP association and thus perform simultanous open. This interface is bi-directional, either side can run the same procedures with their peer as it is doing mobility procedures in either direction between two radio bases station.  

Thus, both are initiators and will continue into key-management as DTLS Clients. Thus, you would need to resolve this tie such that you know that the DTLS message you receive is not a servers response to your client request, but instead a client request to your server. This is clearly not a problem for SCTP association where one have a clear client and server role in the higher protocol.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
Interesting. My recollection was that we don't need to support INIT collision. We make an explicit statement about this at the end of section 1:
```
   Applications using the DTLS chunk can leverage most transport
   features provided by SCTP and its extensions.  However, the following
   limitations apply:

   *  The handling of INIT collisions is not supported.

   *  Performing an SCTP restart without knowing the restart key
      material is not supported.

   *  The use of the lookup address in the Dynamic Address
      Reconfiguration extension as specified in [RFC5061] is not
      supported.
```
Is this not true anymore?

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
We are investigating impact of simultaneous open on the 3GPP interface. 

I do think we will need to consider this issue more than what we have so far. The reason is that we need to make it clear to SCTP implementers if they need to prevent simultaneous open from working if one include DTLS chunk parameters, or if we can provide a clear guidance so that key-management can handle this situation correctly. Because I do think if we just declare it not supported we end up with a rather bad failure mode:

Endpoints A and B do simultaneous open against each other. Both stacks supports this. The both establish this, but the higher layer both think they have the client role. @tuexen are there any Socket API indication that a simultaneous open has happened, or will this just look like a normal open? 

If both endpoint has no indication that this is a simultaneous open then we will a client role mismatch on the key-management layer unless we make it very explicit that they need to be ready for this to happen. 

I think the issue that do exist around this is if the DTLS key-management method parameter lists may not result in the same outcome. I think the core here is that unless A and B have the same list, the two endpoints will have different offered list, even if they both select the same method in the end. Thus resulting in key-derivation failures. 

I don't think we can leave this as is, we need to deal with it in some way.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
> We are investigating impact of simultaneous open on the 3GPP interface.
OK. But this is a change of requirements.

DTLS has a clear client and server role. So you need to define which side has which role. In most implementations also the code is different.
> 
> I do think we will need to consider this issue more than what we have so far. The reason is that we need to make it clear to SCTP implementers if they need to prevent simultaneous open from working if one include DTLS chunk parameters, or if we can provide a clear guidance so that key-management can handle this situation correctly. Because I do think if we just declare it not supported we end up with a rather bad failure mode:
> 
> Endpoints A and B do simultaneous open against each other. Both stacks supports this. The both establish this, but the higher layer both think they have the client role. [@tuexen](https://github.com/tuexen) are there any Socket API 
indication that a simultaneous open has happened, or will this just look like a normal open?

You don't get such an indication. And it is not relevant for the upper layer. It is only important that the collision only results in  a single SCTP association.
> 
> If both endpoint has no indication that this is a simultaneous open then we will a client role mismatch on the key-management layer unless we make it very explicit that they need to be ready for this to happen.

> 
> I think the issue that do exist around this is if the DTLS key-management method parameter lists may not result in the same outcome. I think the core here is that unless A and B have the same list, the two endpoints will have different offered list, even if they both select the same method in the end. Thus resulting in key-derivation failures.

Sure. The current solution works only under the assumption that no INIT collision happens. If you change now that requirement, we need to change the handling of the Key Management Parameter in the handshake. We can make sure that both sides get a consistent view.

Are you saying that the 3GPP interface has no notion of different roles? Both sides are the same? Why was this changed?

> 
> I don't think we can leave this as is, we need to deal with it in some way.

Any more late changes of requirements of the solution we are working on?

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
Hi,
remaining on the 3GPP area, the problem of race condition when opening the Client Level protocol, in that case X2, X2 etc. is solved by those level and poses no problems.
Existing protocols initiate the Client Level handshake with a mechanism like INIT/INIT-ACK but take no actions in case of race condition, and that works well for them.
It may be worthful to write something about in the draft.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body: OK. Let me think about this. Maybe we can solve this problem and the solution has one restriction less. Let us talk about this at the next VC.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
Yes, lets discuss. 

I think there are two issue here:
1) Ensure that both endpoints agree on whose offer and selection of key-management method both should use. 
2) Indicate through the API which side was considered as the "initiator" so that the key-management easily without a lot of complexity trigger the right procedures.

I would think this could easily be done on the initiator tags values. The probability of collision is so low that if one forces a association close and retry if that happens it is not an issue. The probability of two randomly picked 32-bit values colliding is low enough.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
I am thinking about delegating the decision to the key management layer but provide an API to do the decision. The API would be to expose the verification tags. I think we can even avoid the collision case.
This assumes:

- There is no middlebox interfering with the verification tags
- It does not matter if an end point forces itself in the DTLS client or server role.

I think both are fine.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
So I agree that if one provide a signal to the key-management side to figure out its role that would be sufficient assuming that the key-management method offered and selected provides the same answer. I think this is where the issue arise as to my understanding if we have two endpoints A and B and they have different offered lists the endpoints have to select which set of INIT and INIT-ACK to use to represent the offered and selected. 

A offers (1,3,4) in its INIT.
B offers (4,2,1) in its INIT
B will respond with an INIT ACK with 1 or 4 depending on its implementation which preference it holds higher. Lets say 4
A will respond with an INIT ACK with 1 or 4 depending on its implementation. Lets say 1

Each endpoint on receiving INIT ACK will send the Cookie ECHO, and depending on timing the endpoints will enter ESTABLISHED based on receiving Cookie ECHO or COOKIE ACK. However we do have two different sets of offered, selected here:

A's initiation results in the offered+slected list of: 1,3,4,4
B's initiation becomes: 4,2,1,1. 

So in this case both A and B needs to select the same list to use. Thus I think we select as considered initiator the one with the highest Initiation Tag. Thus, the SCTP implementation can deliver a single response for the key-managmenet method offered+selected list, and indicates who was the initiator in this session. 

If the higher layer is expected to do this then you have to deliver both lists when they exists and the tags. Isn't that more complex than to let the SCTP Stack provides a hopefully uniform result through the API? 

Michael, are there any case where one endpoint will have both lists and the other only one due to timing and still both consider it to be established? Because that would be problematic if one select a list and the whole was never delivered. But, I assume that means that although both sides sent INIT, only one progressed through the whole process. For example if B's Init ACK in response to A-> INIT is lost, then one will never perform the process in RFC 9260 Section 5.2.4. 

I think we need a couple of diagrams showing alternatives of what happens. Because this rule requires both sides to know that its peer have seen the INIT-ACK. Thus A, can only apply it if it has received an Cookie ECHO from B, but that is the requirement to enter Established anyway. So if A sent Cookie ECHO and then receives and Cookie ECHO from B it enters established, but B never receives A's Cookie ECHO, it will either fire its (B) T1 timer, or receive the Cookie ACK for its Cookie ECHO. However, in this situation B does not know if A received the INIT ACK from B as response to A's INIT. And as A has entered established it will not resend its COOKIE ECHO.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
@teiclap and I had a white boarding session and came up with a design which we think works for all cases. First of all we need to make clear that we need to preserve some capabilities here.

Client/Server interaction where the server will never open an association must not be impacted as a role reversal would not work from a security perspective, as potentially only the server have a certificate identity, so the client can't act as key-management layer server. 

We also need to deal with the simultaneous open cases, also when there are losses of messages during the handshake. Based on this we came up with fours pieces of a solution. 

1) Change the Key-Management method parameter to only be used as each side declaring its preference list of methods. 
2) Define a Init collision state for the SCTP association. The core here is that we need to ensure that each side enters this if we have a simultaneous open case. I think we have something that might work in most cases, but I do have some questions about state transition to see if this is an issue or not. 
3) In case of an INIT collision state has been triggered. Then the two SCTP endpoints resolve their roles by comparing their Initialization tags. The one with lower value is considered the initiator of the SCTP association.
4) A deterministic algorithm that takes the two endpoint's Key-management preference lists and determine the offered and selected method from these two. This algorithm I think is as simple as two step process:
       4A) Determine commonly supported methods between the two endpoint's preference lists.
       4B) Select the most preferred method out of the common ones for the endpoint that was determined to be the responder (server). 
       4C) Create the offered+selected list, by combining the Initiator role's Offered preference list, and the select method from 4B.

Then we do a tweak to the API to include in the abstract API's "Get Offered DTLS Key Management Methods" to include the indicator of collision and which role this endpoint was selected. The offered+selected list is provided based on 4. 

I think all these steps are straight forward. Where we are uncertain if one 2) that we have an method that does not contain issues. Thus I would hope that @tuexen can help clarify what really happens in a particular case when one perform simultaneous open.

So the case is the following:

A: Sends INIT
B: Send INIT 
A: Receives INIT and Sends INIT ACK
B: Receives A's INIT and Sends A an INIT ACK
Network drops A's INIT ACK to B.
A: Receives INIT ACK and Sends Cookie ECHO
B: Receives Cookie ECHO

What does B do at this stage. Will it enters Established as it received its valid Cookie, or will it not enter Established as it is not in Cookie WAIT as it hasn't received A's Cookie? In case of the later Then B's T1 timer will fire and B will retransmit it's INIT, and hopefully receive an INIT ACK and thus Send Cookie ECHO which result in it entering Cookie ECHOED, in which allows it to enter ESTABLISHED based on Option D per Section 5.2.4 of RFC 9260. 

So for the step 2 process to work reliable each peer should enter collision state when it has received an INIT from the peer that matches the process of 5.2.1 in RFC 9260, but only if it knows that the peer will also enter this state, which do requires each endpoint to see an INIT ACK from its peer in response to its sent INIT. 

Thus this process is very dependent on if an endpoint will enter ESTABLISHED from receiving a valid cookie in COOKIE-WAIT state when simultaneous open case is occurring. I will note that to my understanding if one enters collision state when receiving INIT, this can become one sided in case the endpoint that received an INIT had its INIT being lost by the network before it arrived at the peer.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
> We are investigating impact of simultaneous open on the 3GPP interface.
> 
> I do think we will need to consider this issue more than what we have so far. The reason is that we need to make it clear to SCTP implementers if they need to prevent simultaneous open from working if one include DTLS chunk parameters, or if we can provide a clear guidance so that key-management can handle this situation correctly. Because I do think if we just declare it not supported we end up with a rather bad failure mode:
> 
> Endpoints A and B do simultaneous open against each other. Both stacks supports this. The both establish this, but the higher layer both think they have the client role. [@tuexen](https://github.com/tuexen) are there any Socket API indication that a simultaneous open has happened, or will this just look like a normal open?

A simultaneous open is not indicated in the socket API. Only if it is a restart: [RFC 6458](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6458#section-6.1.1). `sac_state` would be `SCTP_RESTART`.
> 
> If both endpoint has no indication that this is a simultaneous open then we will a client role mismatch on the key-management layer unless we make it very explicit that they need to be ready for this to happen.
> 
> I think the issue that do exist around this is if the DTLS key-management method parameter lists may not result in the same outcome. I think the core here is that unless A and B have the same list, the two endpoints will have different offered list, even if they both select the same method in the end. Thus resulting in key-derivation failures.
> 
> I don't think we can leave this as is, we need to deal with it in some way.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
We probably need to talk a bit about the reason why we need to properly identify Client and Server role.
In the current agreement, we need to have a Client and a Server because of the way we compute the mask used for Key Context extraction.
In the draft, the INIT/INIT-ACK handshake requires that Client sends a list of proposed Methods and the Server replies the one chosen, then the set of proposed methods+the chosen are used uniquely from both peers for Key Context extraction.
Magnus suggests that both Client and Server send the list of proposed methods and then a Method Selection algorithm is used at both peers on the two lists that will provide a unique choice.
A function would provide the method so that  m(list1,list2) = m(list2,list1)
If we start from Magnus' proposal, we can continue on the same approach and add a Mask Selection algorithm that given the two list of methods provides a unique mask.
A function would provide the mask so that  n(list1,list2) = n(list2,list1)

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
I think we have two problems to solve:
1. Negotiate the cryptographic algorithms used for the DTLS chunk.
2. Run the DTLS handshake in the key management layer.

Originally, we assumed a client/server role for the SCTP handshake and derived from that solutions for the above problems. If we do not require the SCTP client and server roles anymore, we need different solutions. I think for the first problem it doesn't matter how the algorithm is selected as long as it is done consistently. For the second problem we need to define if each end can support client and server roles for DTLS or not. Here we need to be clear on the requirements of the solution before we discuss a specific solution.
Let us discuss this in detail during the VC.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
Part of the issue is that we have both cases here:

Client Server application where a server would never initiated the SCTP association so a simultaneous open will never occur. Also in these cases I would also expect that the server has a well defined identity used in the key-management. The client may practice mutual authentication in this application, but there can also be application that do not need to have an identity. 

Then we have applications like the Xn Interface where we have nodes that need PtP associations between each other where either side can open a new association when need arise. In these simultanous open may occur. On the key-management level these likely are performing mutual authentication. So they can likely do either role when it comes to key-management but they would need to know to avoid additional complexities with both sides opening key-management. The above assuming that both endpoints agree on the method, i.e. SCTP API should deliver one common result for both endpoints, both for initiating the right method and secondly provide the same key-deriviation string.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: This issue has now been resolved now that we have updated the DTLS Key Management Parameter and specified how the role is decided from it. Relevant PRs are: #118 #119 #122

issues.title: Define which DTLS Sequence Number the restart DTLS Key Context is initiated to
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
Part of the known state necessary for SCTP Restart with DTLS Chunk to work is what is the first DTLS sequence number will be used when sedning restart DTLS key context protected data. This appear to be currently missing, but should be defined.

- A never used restart context starts from a given sequence number. 
- When a restart context have been used, even if the restart failed the restart DTLS Key Context needs to be invalidated as it can't be resused for a second attempt without resulting in using the same nonce twice.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
The sequence number will start from 0 per Section 4.2 of https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-tls-rfc9147bis-01.html#name-sequence-number-and-epoch

From a sequence number aspect as long as one are inside the peers replay window incrementing the sequence number works. The real danger here with restart during the restart process is if the restarting node not correctly peristantly store the sequence numbers used. Then a two time pad can occur. So I think it is basically  a security consideration that if one have attempted to do a restart that should immediately be recorded in one persistant state so that node do not attempt a restart.

issues.title: Clarify how primary and restart key context interact with DTLS sequence number and replay window
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body: The primary and restart DTLS key context have independent state for DTLS record sequence numbers as well as replay window. While concurrent sets of the DTLS Key Contexts for primary context interact with the same sequence number and replay window. This should be made clearer in the draft to avoid confusion.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Realize that I mispoke about the sequence number. The sequence number is per Epoch, thus being reset for each new epoch. So the replay window is independent. However, the difference here between primary and restart might need some improvement.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Review if resolved.

issues.title: How to deal with attacks on Restart
issues.author: teiclap
issues.body:
The current assumption is that as soon as INIT/INIT-ACK COOKIE-ECHO/COOKIE-ACK for Restart purposes is completed, the Endpoint  will start sending DATA by using the Restart DTLS Key Context. Restart Key Context is assumed to be persistently and securely stored somewhere.

A malicious attacker that has succeeded getting a copy of Restart DTLS Key Context MAY succeed hijacking the secure Association by issuing a protected SCTP Restart, and since we permit DATA to use the Restart DTLS Key Context, he would be able encrypting and decrypting data until the Endpoints will not rekey. Since the malicious user misses the other secrets (Certificates), he won't succeed rekeying, still the Association will continue with the Restart DTLS Key Context as we didn't specify how to deal with this case.

In my opinion, we SHOULD prohibit using Restart DTLS Key Context for DATA, limiting the use for new key negotiation. That SHOULD lead the Association to be closed in some ways when rekey would fail for bad Authoring.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
@teiclap and I had some discussion of this issue.

First of all the DTLS Chunk draft is very clear on that if one does not keep the restart security context as secure one can be subject to association hijacking attacks. Assuming the proposed key management methods one have similar need to persistently securely store certificates used in authenticating the endpoint to the peer. If the secret part is lost to an attacker equally the attacker can establish secure associations inpersonating the endpoint. 

Thus this attack needs to be taken as a mitigation if one have been subject to an attack. Depending on the application one might find the proposed solution of not allowing sending any ULP payloads before a new handshake has taken place. But, that means that one accept additional delay after restart. If one do not accept delay then the proposed mitigation might be problematic. 

The next aspect of this is that as we have defined the DTLS Chunk and its API it will be the functionality on top of the API that implements the key management methods that will be responsible for blocking any data to the ULP. Thus, any actual implementation of the proposed mitigation here would fall onto that implemenation. 

This leads to the conclusion that at most we should consider strengthening the security consideration. However, persoanally I am hesitant to spend security consideration on something that attempts to mitigate a case where security has already failed due to another implementation error. It is more important that the implementors spend time securing the restart keys properly.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: Discussed at Design Team meeting. No need to document in chunk.

issues.title: Clarify Negotiate meaning here.
issues.author: gloinul
issues.body:
SCTP Considerations currently states:

The SCTP authentication extension (SCTP-AUTH) defined in [[RFC4895](file:///Users/eramswd/ietf/draft-westerlund-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-chunk/draft-ietf-tsvwg-sctp-dtls-chunk.html#RFC4895)] is incompatible with the extension defined in this document. Therefore, its support MUST NOT be negotiated in combination with the support of the DTLS chunk.

Reading this a possible interpretation is that one is not allowed to offer SCTP-AUTH parameters in an INIT. I think this would be the wrong interpretation. What is required is that the INIT-ACK need to have chosen between SCTP-AUTH or DTLS Key Management Parameters. 

Do you agree and should this paragraph be reformulated?

While working on this issue, I came to the conclusion that it might not be a good idea to write something like "MUST NOT retry unless condition". An on-path attacker could just intercept the initial handshake, enforce such a role conflict by changing a single packet, and as a consequence block the communication for a long time.
So I think we should not make such a statement. See #134.

comments.author: gloinul

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
Most likely it is a configuration error. Something similar to configure both sides a servers. If it is an attacker, the impact is limited. Somewhere to dropping packets or modifying them. There are plenty of ways to get the same result. So I think we don't need some explicit description of this case.
If we want a description, we should cover the general possibility to modify the packets in a way that the SCTP association setup succeeds, but the DTLS chunk support fails due to the attacker.
But again: The cause can always be a mis-configuration or an attacker.

issues.title: How does this interact with WARP/SNAP?
issues.author: ValorZard
issues.body:
From what I can tell, this proposal implements embedding DTLS in SCTP (probably for WebRTC purposes if I had to guess)

However, there is a separate proposal, SNAP, being implemented that also does the same thing?

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-hancke-tsvwg-snap/

This is part of the WARP effort:

https://github.com/fippo/warp-snap-sped
https://github.com/pion/webrtc/issues/3335

Are these two complementary, or do they do completely different things.

comments.author: gloinul
ValorZard
comments.body:
Ah i see.

So, this almost feels like there will be two diverging forms of SCTP at some point.

Native SCTP (that would implement this proposal)

And WebRTC SCTP (that would implement SNAP)

I feel like this could get unwieldy fast, is there any solution for this divergence or is that just the way these standards work over time?

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
SCTP has a fair number of extensions. Some of them are not applicable in the WebRTC context, like for example (RFC 5061 - Stream Control Transmission Protocol (SCTP) Dynamic Address Reconfiguration) [https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/rfc5061/]. 

At the same time the mobile network signalling use cases has other set of constraints when it comes to deploying solutions and extensions. Resulting in that DTLS Chunk is the most appropriate solution we have found after several years of working on a solution that resolved the user message size limitation of RFC 6083 and fixes the security issues found e.g. with SCTP-AUTH.

Yes, there are some divergence, most to me related to how one deploys SCTP. So I don't think there are real divergence within a particular application/use case. Also I don't think it is happening fast, please remember that it has been over 25 years since SCTP first was published as an RFC. It is more that WebRTC is a very special use case where one have been willing to do some particular choices to make things work and then optimize them in the SNAP and SPED proposals.

A restarts: and send init
B sends INIT-ACK
A sends Cookie ECHO (R3-O)
B Send Cookie ACK (R3-O)
New Keymanagement

issues.title: 1-byte Pre-padding Always Results in 3-byte Post-padding
issues.author: lxin
issues.body:
The current DTLS chunk layout is:

```
DTLS chunk header (4 bytes) |
Pre-padding | unified_hdr | seq_num (1 + 1 + 2 bytes) |
SCTP Common chunks (4 * n bytes) |
Centent_type (1 bytes) |
Tag_length (16 bytes) |
Post-padding (3 bytes) <---
```

Is this 'fixed' 3-byte Post-padding expected in DTLS chunk? It appears to introduce unnecessary bandwidth overhead.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: So not all cipher algorithms have 16 bytes long long authentication tag lengths. But with any multiple of 4 of four bytes I guess this is unfortunate consequence of providing 32-bit aligned start location for the cipher text.

comments.author: lxin
comments.body:
> So not all cipher algorithms have 16 bytes long long authentication tag lengths. But with any multiple of 4 of four bytes I guess this is unfortunate consequence of providing 32-bit aligned start location for the cipher text.

Just let you know, the tag length of all the ciphers in TLS 1.3 are 4-bytes aligned:

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8446#appendix-B.4

and if we delete the 1-byte pre-padding, we will not need 3-byte post-padding, and it saves 4 bytes in DTLS payload for user data. :-)

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: But then the DTLS records start of the cipher text will not be 32-byte aligned. Which will force BSD to do something here to handle the ciphering efficiently. So which is more important here, saving 4 bytes or having 32-bit alignment for in place ciphering I guess we have to discuss.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: The implementation performance impact of not having the SCTP 32-bit alignment is significant, and can on some CPU architectures cause crashes. Thus we will have to accept the 4-byte overhead.

pulls.body:
Resolves #23 and #24.
