comments.body: I think this should be done, but is related to association restart.

Potential grammatical issue "those connection". However, I think we need to agree on a term for what a key + plus rest of context should be called. It is not a full DTLS connection. DTLS key context? A DTLS Key Context is the state related to a specific set of keys, the replay window, next DTLS sequence number, and its identifying values: Restart bit, DTLS connection ID (If used), and epoch.
Note, I think the definition I write above should be added elsewhere and used here instead of connection.
I think instead of using /, list all chunk types in a comma separated list. Also rewrite using "restart DTLS key context".
I think you need to expand this to say: A DTLS Chunk using the restart DTLS key context is identified ... then remove the tail end "in the DTLS chunk"
I think this should use [DTLS CHUNK(INIT)] and [DTLS CHUNK(INIT-ACK)]

I don't think we should redefine the Chunk Length, even if it is the only Chunk we should ever see on this level. The sum of the chunk length fields are the valid SCTP packet length. Although one might derive this length from the IP header I wonder if it is smart to redefine the field.
With the new derivation based solution, I think we expect the restart keys to be configured as soon as the main keys have been installed for each direction.
So I think this happens earlier than PROTECTED state. We might need to rework the state machine transitions slightly.
The API may need to be clarified that a given key are valid for the combination of (SCTP association, DTLS Connection ID (if used), epoch and restart bit).
You are right, there are no connections, those are Keys
I think we should use the word context, as it is not only keys, there are the replay window, latest used sequence number as well as the context identifiers.
MT was quite peculiar on removing this. We can talk about
We have some work to do here, agree. I am not changing this for now but we know that Keys are derived at once and the state machine requires fine tuning
But isn't it context a word with a precise semantic in DTLS? I have nothing against context, as long as it makes easier reading

I think we have to ask him, I don't remember him talking about redefining the chunk length field. He tried to optimize things. The length field in the DTLS record is optional, and should never be needed when using DTLS chunk as the DTLS chunk length field exists. There is a clear downside I think to make the DTLS chunk field not match the standard definition. Any firewall that sees a length field that is zero while the actual length is non-zero will react to the packet being errnous and might discard it.

Regarding the DTLS 1.3 specification. The word context is used only three times, where one is part of a reference. So I think it is fine to define "DTLS Key Context" having the meaning we want it to have here to use the DTLS record like in a DTLS connection.
This is a good point.
I am restoring the length

Then let's have it added in the definition.
DTLS Key Context is the set of data defining a Key and it's applicability, such as epoch, sequence number etc.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
Question: What is the intended behavior if one side wants to use the DTLS chunk and the other not? I see two options:
1. Get the association up and the application decide whether it is acceptable to continue without the protection or not.
2. Ensure that the SCTP layer prohibits the establishment of the association.
If we can to use the second option, the response to an INIT chunk can't be an ERROR chunk but must be an ABORT chunk.

comments.author: teiclap
comments.body:
> Question: What is the intended behavior if one side wants to use the DTLS chunk and the other not? I see two options:
> 
> 1. Get the association up and the application decide whether it is acceptable to continue without the protection or not.
> 2. Ensure that the SCTP layer prohibits the establishment of the association.
>    If we can to use the second option, the response to an INIT chunk can't be an ERROR chunk but must be an ABORT chunk.

I was wondering if the correct behavior would rather be that if the Endpoint doesn't accept unprotected INIT, it may silently discard it.

Anyhow I don't support 1. I can easily fix 2.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: This is where the server endpoint should have a policy set that determine if the ULP requires DTLS security or can accept no security or some other fallback. So if not required the association should be established without the DTLS chunk, if required then the policy not meet error should be returned.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body: I would also note that we need too be able to return both "Policy not met", "No common protection solution" as the later issue triggers the first. Where the later can if there is no require DTLS Chunk policy is in place can be returned as just an error to indicate why DTLS chunk was not returned, but the association can be established.

comments.body:
I think this text is not consistent with the current terminology. There is no `Protection Operator` anymore. In this document the initial association setup will be always in plain text. Then upper layer messages will be exchanged and at some point the upper layer will set client and server keys and enforce the reception of DTLS chunks only.

I think we need one specific section describing the SCTP handshake (for the initial one and the restart one). Another section should describe how to send/recv packets before and after setting the client and server keys and enforcing the reception of DTLS chunks only.

I think the handling of packets is clear and we have a common understanding. I have been contemplating about the handshake for a couple of days and I think we do not have a common understand right now. I am sure we can get one, but I would like to discuss this in person at the IETF with you (or in a VC if you prefer).

comments.body: I think it is to strong wording to recommend not using the Connection ID (C bit). We support it so we need to be more neutral. It clearly is more easy to calculate the header size if it is not used.
I wonder if it would be better to reference the relevant section in the specification where we define that multiple DTLS records are not used, rather than repeat the normative language.
I think what needs to be added here, is that if the connection ID is used, then the length of it needs to be retrived from the key context.
If we are going to have the calculation here then we should include if C bit is set. Also I think the calculation of P needs to be generalize to enable for cases where size is larger than 4.
Recommending not to use C makes the computation much easier and quicker. Also we have no reason to use C-Id as we have other ways for recognizing the connection. I'd like to get rid of C-Id.
As size is bit-handed with 0x3 it's never greater than 3.
I think this is unclear as (4-size) could become a signed integer result (minus something) and thus erroneous. If you change this to "4-(size & 0x03)" then I am convinced that it will work.

I meant the calculation of "size" as
size = (1 + (B & 0x08) ? 1 : 2 + (B & 0x04) ? 0 : 2) & 0x3
is always <= 3
I fail to find this somewhere else. So lets leave it as it is for now.

pulls.author: tuexen
pulls.body:
There is one point we need to discuss in the VC:

When is the role and the method being determined. The text says "after the INIT ACK has been sent or received" but wouldn't it be better to state something like "when the association enters the ESTABLISHED state?

Depending on this, I need to update the selection of tie-breakers.

comments.author: gloinul
comments.body:
Regarding the question if the selection procedure should be run after the init ack been sent and received, or use when established. I think the current is actually unoptimal and should be addressed in either direction.

When Established: Running the determination when entering established state have the advantage of being clear and is a good point in time for the key-management implementation on top of the SCTP stack. The down side is that in some of the failure cases one will establish an SCTP association that will then be aborted by the determination process. These cases are:
  1. Incompatible roles
  2. No common key methods
  3. Tie breaker collision

Thus, if one want to avoid establishing the association before tearing it down then SCTP responder should in fact analyze this prior to sending the INIT-ACK. And if any of these occur send a ABORT instead of the INIT-ACK. This is the same that will happen if the responder requires DTLS key-management parameter and the INIT does not contain one. Thus avoiding that endpoint holding any state at all. 

I will also note that in the case of the Initiator receiving a INIT-ACK without the DTLS key-management parameter and requiring support then it will abort the connection prior to sending the COOKIE-ECHO. Which basically just means not sending it and terminating the establishment procedure?

comments.author: lxin
comments.body: Do we really need a new struct for it?  We can pass a buf to getsockopt, both userspace and kernel will parse it as uint8_t (*)[2], and the optlen pointer in getsockopt() will tell userspace how many SUITES the optval contains.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body:
> Do we really need a new struct for it? We can pass a buf to getsockopt, both userspace and kernel will parse it as uint8_t (*)[2], and the optlen pointer in getsockopt() will tell userspace how many SUITES the optval contains.

This only works if you know how much cipher suites are there. Having a number in the struct, you can provide a 2 byte buffer, get the number and call a second time with a correctly sized buffer. Without that you would have to retry until `*opt_len` on return is smaller than `*opt_len` on call.

comments.author: lxin
comments.body:
> > Do we really need a new struct for it? We can pass a buf to getsockopt, both userspace and kernel will parse it as uint8_t (*)[2], and the optlen pointer in getsockopt() will tell userspace how many SUITES the optval contains.
> 
> This only works if you know how much cipher suites are there. Having a number in the struct, you can provide a 2 byte buffer, get the number and call a second time with a correctly sized buffer. Without that you would have to retry until `*opt_len` on return is smaller than `*opt_len` on call.

*opt_len can also do this trick, return err with *opt_len set to the real buffer size required when the optval is too small.

If there's assoc_id needed, I think it will be good to have a new struct, like others. Or do you know any similar existing case without assoc_id needed?

comments.body:
> > > Do we really need a new struct for it? We can pass a buf to getsockopt, both userspace and kernel will parse it as uint8_t (*)[2], and the optlen pointer in getsockopt() will tell userspace how many SUITES the optval contains.

> 
> *opt_len can also do this trick, return err with *opt_len set to the real buffer size required when the optval is too small.

That is Linux specific and not described in [Posix](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9799919799/functions/getsockopt.html). Therefore I would like to avoid using that.

> 
> If there's assoc_id needed, I think it will be good to have a new struct, like others. Or do you know any similar existing case without assoc_id needed?

The point is that we even don't need a socket here. The cipher suites available are a property of the SCTP stack, not the particular endpoint. That is the reason I changed the interface to the functions `sctp_dtls_nr_cipher_suites()` and `sctp_dtls_cipher_suites()`. They did not use a file descriptor argument... So using a socket option is already not a perfect fit...

> > > If there's assoc_id needed, I think it will be good to have a new struct, like others. Or do you know any similar existing case without assoc_id needed?
> > 
> > 
> > The point is that we even don't need a socket here. The cipher suites available are a property of the SCTP stack, not the particular endpoint. That is the reason I changed the interface to the functions `sctp_dtls_nr_cipher_suites()` and `sctp_dtls_cipher_suites()`. They did not use a file descriptor argument... So using a socket option is already not a perfect fit...
> 
> OK. If so, maybe we should leave it to a sysctl option? like TCP's congestion available algos in linux:
> 
> ```
> # sysctl net.ipv4.tcp_available_congestion_control
> net.ipv4.tcp_available_congestion_control = reno cubic bbr
> ```

We can do that, but that might not be portable. I think the paths are different and we might use different encodings... My goal was to have an API, which allows portable applications.
For example, the above would use `net.inet.tcp.cc.available` in FreeBSD and `bbr` would not be a CC, but a TCP function block:
```
net.inet.tcp.functions_available: 
Stack                           D Alias                            PCB count
freebsd                         * freebsd                          5
bbr                               bbr                              0
rack                              rack                             0
```
Just to be crystal clear: I never said that `sctp_dtls_nr_cipher_suites()` and `sctp_dtls_cipher_suites()` need to be system calls. They can be wrapper around the `sysctl`-interface. Or around an unspecified socket option. Or something else...
In FreeBSD this would go into libc, on Linux I think you have an SCTP specific library you can link against.

comments.author: tuexen
comments.body: This resolves #143.

I think this overly simplified and can result in that the restart key are used when it shouldn't be used. Thus potentially resulting in two-time pad if the sending endpoint do restart. I also think it might not actually work as a rule when responding to restarting endpoints as you will have the primary key and the restart key. 

So for the non-restarting endpoint. It has both installed, should use the primary when it exist except:
- When responding to cookie-echo with cookie-ack => results in it creating a new association with the restart DTLS Key context copied over. 

In an assocation resulting from the above restart the above rule will work as it will have a restart context being used until a new primary is being set. 

On the restarting side, it needs to set the restart keys prior to call "connect" in the API. 

If I interpret this correctly what we should maybe introduce is a rule that in the API the only time one can set the restart keys without first having set primary keys (for that epoch?) is when the association doesn't exist.

You are correct, when an endpoint detects that the peer has restarted (when processing the COOKIE chunk) the restart key has to be transferred. Let me double check the text describing the restart if it already contains this or provide some tweaks.
For the API restriction, I would limit that to the send keys.
Agree, only sends keys would need restrictions to help ensure that the restart key are not wrongly used.
Added clarifications. OK?
Yes

L1094 : It's not clear for me.

L1209 : this poses quite some problems as it cannot be KM to decide when using R Keys. At reception of R-protected COOKIE-ECHO, that doesn't arrive to  KM, the Association must automatically switch to encoding output traffic, thus COOKIE-ACK, as R-protected. KM will only know that there's a need for new handshake when receiving clientHello from the restarting peer.
